title: Thinking in React, State Management, and Derived State

learningObjectives.text: Trace and explain thinking in react workflow using a new example and justify the result.
Explain placing state using a new example and justify the result.
Explain lifting state using a new example and justify the result.
Explain derived state using a new example and justify the result.

whyThisMatters: The ideas in thinking in react, state management, and derived state recur throughout later React work, reducing memorization and debugging later. It belongs to the **State and Thinking in React** module.
mentalModel: Draw a decision map: **Thinking in React workflow** → **Placing state** → **Lifting state** → **Derived state**. Add branches where the learner must choose an alternative and label the evidence for the choice.

sections.title: Thinking in React workflow
sections.body: Start from the data model and static component tree, identify minimal state, choose its owner, and add upward communication through callbacks.

sections.title: Placing state
sections.body: Place state in the lowest component that owns all consumers and updates. Move it upward or into a shared mechanism only when ownership demands it.

sections.title: Lifting state
sections.body: Lifting state moves shared state to the closest common parent, giving siblings one source of truth through props and callbacks.

sections.title: Derived state
sections.body: Values calculable from props and state should usually be derived during render rather than stored separately. Duplicate state creates synchronization bugs.

sections.title: Worked example
sections.explanation: The best score is derived during render instead of duplicated in state.

commonMistakes: Mutating props or state instead of producing the next value.
Adding state or an Effect for a value that can be derived during render.
Using global state before identifying the real owner and consumers.
Optimizing renders without a measured user-facing problem.
summary: Thinking in React, State Management, and Derived State connects Thinking in React workflow, Placing state, Lifting state.
Use the topic to make behavior and decisions explicit rather than memorizing isolated syntax.
Verify understanding by explaining a new example and answering the topic quiz without looking back.

topicQuiz.failurePolicy: Show explanations, add missed objectives to local review, and allow immediate or later retry.

choices.feedback: Thinking in React workflow is best understood as follows: Start from the data model and static component tree, identify minimal state, choose its owner, and add upward communication through callbacks.

choices.label: Use an Effect for synchronization caused by rendering. Derived calculations belong in render and action-specific logic belongs in event handlers.

choices.label: CSS Modules locally scope class names while retaining standard CSS syntax.
choices.feedback: This does not match the lesson's model. Reconsider the role of Thinking in React workflow.

choices.label: Class lifecycle methods split setup, update, and teardown by timing; Effects group setup and cleanup around one synchronization concern.
choices.feedback: This does not match the lesson's model. Reconsider the role of Thinking in React workflow.

questions.prompt: A learner must use Thinking in React, State Management, and Derived State in a new situation. Which approach best demonstrates transferable understanding?

choices.label: Treat the concept as isolated trivia and avoid connecting it to earlier prerequisites.
choices.feedback: This does not match the lesson's model. Reconsider the role of Thinking in React, State Management, and Derived State.

choices.label: Skip the underlying model and rely on memorized syntax, even when the result is unclear.
choices.feedback: This does not match the lesson's model. Reconsider the role of Thinking in React, State Management, and Derived State.

choices.label: Apply Thinking in React workflow deliberately, make the relevant inputs and assumptions explicit, and verify the observable result before adding more complexity.
choices.feedback: Transfer comes from using the concept with explicit assumptions and checking the resulting behavior, not from reproducing a memorized example.

choices.label: Add more state and abstraction immediately, before identifying the smallest requirement.
choices.feedback: This does not match the lesson's model. Reconsider the role of Thinking in React, State Management, and Derived State.

questions.explanation: Transfer comes from using the concept with explicit assumptions and checking the resulting behavior, not from reproducing a memorized example.
questions.hint: Choose the option that would still work when the surrounding example changes.

questions.prompt: Which sequence is most reliable when solving a problem involving Thinking in React, State Management, and Derived State?

choices.label: Optimize first, then decide what the code is supposed to do.
choices.feedback: This does not match the lesson's model. Reconsider the role of Thinking in React, State Management, and Derived State.

choices.label: First identify the requirement, then apply the relevant rule from Thinking in React workflow, inspect the result, and only then refactor or optimize.
choices.feedback: The reliable sequence starts from the requirement, applies a known rule, observes behavior, and improves the solution afterward.

choices.label: Copy an unrelated implementation, hide the assumptions, and test only the happy path.
choices.feedback: This does not match the lesson's model. Reconsider the role of Thinking in React, State Management, and Derived State.

choices.label: Start with a framework abstraction and avoid checking the underlying language behavior.
choices.feedback: This does not match the lesson's model. Reconsider the role of Thinking in React, State Management, and Derived State.

questions.explanation: The reliable sequence starts from the requirement, applies a known rule, observes behavior, and improves the solution afterward.
questions.hint: Prefer a sequence that makes failures visible early.

questions.prompt: Which guideline shows the best judgment about when to use Thinking in React, State Management, and Derived State?

choices.label: Choose based only on line count, without considering readability, correctness, or future change.
choices.feedback: This does not match the lesson's model. Reconsider the role of Thinking in React, State Management, and Derived State.

choices.label: Use Thinking in React, State Management, and Derived State when it makes intent, ownership, or data flow clearer; avoid adding it mechanically when a simpler, equally explicit solution is sufficient.
choices.feedback: Engineering judgment balances clarity and cost. The concept should serve the requirement rather than become an end in itself.

choices.label: Avoid Thinking in React, State Management, and Derived State completely because all abstractions reduce maintainability.
choices.feedback: This does not match the lesson's model. Reconsider the role of Thinking in React, State Management, and Derived State.

choices.label: Use Thinking in React, State Management, and Derived State in every file because more abstraction is always better.
choices.feedback: This does not match the lesson's model. Reconsider the role of Thinking in React, State Management, and Derived State.

questions.explanation: Engineering judgment balances clarity and cost. The concept should serve the requirement rather than become an end in itself.
questions.hint: Choose the option that acknowledges both benefits and trade-offs.
